title: Marriage, Faith, and Family: How JD Vance Became the Father He Never Had
gist: Using VP JD Vance's memoirs as a case study, the essay argues America faces a fatherhood crisis driven most by men who never become fathers, and that marriage, faith and supportive community are what convert at-risk men into engaged fathers.

insights.claim: The number of childless men ages 25-45 has risen from 10 million in 1980 to 23 million in 2024, a record high, per Grant Bailey and Brad Wilcox.
insights.speaker: Institute for Family Studies
insights.so_what: A more than doubling of childless prime-age men quantifies the demand-side collapse behind falling fertility.
insights.quote: The number of men ages 25-45 who are childless has risen from 10 million in 1980 to 23 million in 2024, a record high

insights.claim: Men who do become fathers now invest more time and energy in their children than ever, even as the ranks of childless men swell.
insights.speaker: Institute for Family Studies
insights.so_what: A bifurcation: engaged dads intensify investment while a growing cohort opts out entirely, shaping child-related consumption.

insights.claim: The rise of childless men reflects self-selection: men from good family backgrounds choose fatherhood while men from adverse family situations often feel unequipped and bypass family life.
insights.speaker: Institute for Family Studies
insights.so_what: Suggests intergenerational transmission of family breakdown, a compounding driver of declining family formation.
insights.locator: While reasons for the rise of childless men

insights.claim: Marriage itself facilitates better fatherhood, especially for boys, per Brad Wilcox's 'Married Fathers Matter' argument.
insights.speaker: Institute for Family Studies
insights.so_what: Positions marriage as the mechanism, not just correlate, converting men into engaged fathers.

insights.claim: A new study finds boys who do not reside with married fathers are more likely to go to prison than graduate from college, while boys from intact married families are markedly more likely to graduate than be jailed.
insights.speaker: Institute for Family Studies
insights.so_what: A stark outcome gap tying family structure to human-capital formation and incarceration costs.
insights.locator: boys who reside with their married fathers

themes.theme: Faith, community and the case study

insights.claim: The essay argues church organically provides the 'village' families need — small groups, prayer groups, meal trains and father figures — that isolated modern parents lack.
insights.speaker: Institute for Family Studies
insights.so_what: Frames religious institutions as social infrastructure for family formation, echoing the friendship-and-support fertility thesis.

insights.claim: JD Vance, who grew up fatherless and had a decade of atheism, was brought to engaged fatherhood by exposure to his wife Usha's functional family, his marriage, and a return to church around his son's birth.
insights.speaker: Institute for Family Studies
insights.so_what: A high-profile illustration of the marriage-plus-faith-plus-community pathway the pronatal movement promotes.

insights.claim: The core prescription is that America needs more fathers bonded to their children through marriage and embedded in supportive communities that enable better fathering.
insights.speaker: Institute for Family Studies
insights.so_what: Distills the policy/cultural agenda: marriage and community, not just income, as levers on family formation.
insights.quote: America needs more fathers who are bonded to their children through marriage and who have supportive communities to support better fathering.
